data.title: Granite Ridge Resources: A Look At Its Guidance For 2025
data.description:
Granite Ridge Resources, Inc. (NYSE:GRNT) reported a surge in production during Q4 2024 and provided guidance that calls for further growth to 29,000 BOEPD in average production in 2025.

At current strip prices, this would result in a significant ($74 million) 2025 cash burn after dividends, although Granite Ridge's leverage at the end of 2025 would be reasonable at 0.9x.

I believe that Granite Ridge is likely to maintain its production guidance for 2025, although it may reduce/defer activity later in the year to reduce its cash burn. This would have more of an impact on 2026 production growth.

I now estimate Granite Ridge's value at $7.25 per share in the long term (after 2026) with $70 WTI oil (CL1:COM) and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas (NG1:COM). This is a $1 per share decrease from my prior estimate of its value, reflecting increased projected near-term cash burn and a $5 decrease in my long-term oil price expectations.

Notes On Guidance

Granite Ridge expects approximately 29,000 BOEPD (52% oil) in average production during 2025. This is a 16% increase in total production and a 23% increase in oil production compared to its average 2024 production.

Granite Ridge ended 2024 on a high note with regard to production, with Q4 2024 production at 27,734 BOEPD (53% oil). This was a 10% increase in production compared to Q3 2024 and represents Granite Ridge's highest production level so far.

Granite Ridge's 2025 guidance calls for a more modest 5% increase in total production and a 2% increase in oil production compared to its Q4 2024 averages.

Granite Ridge's 2025 guidance also involves 4% to 5% higher production (both total and oil) compared to what I had modeled for it before. Granite Ridge expects approximately $310 million in 2025 capex, which is slightly higher than the $290 million I had modeled for it before.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Based on 29,000 BOEPD (52% oil) in average 2025 production, Granite Ridge should be able to generate $440 million in revenues at its current strip of nearly $65 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Granite Ridge's 2025 hedges have roughly neutral value at its current strip. It has oil hedges covering the majority of its 2025 oil production, but those are collars with a floor that is slightly below strip prices.

Granite Ridge also has a significant amount of natural gas hedges, with the ceilings of its collars generally slightly above the current strip (except for December 2025).

This leads to a projection that Granite Ridge will end up with $16 million in cash burn before dividends at the current strip.

Granite Ridge's current quarterly dividend of $0.11 per share adds up to $58 million in dividend payments per year.

Cash Burn And Debt

Thus, Granite Ridge's cash burn after dividends maybe $74 million for 2025 if it doesn't change its plans. Granite Ridge had $205 million in credit facility borrowings ($196 million in net debt) at the end of 2024.

It is now projected to end 2025 with $270 million in net debt (0.9x leverage). Granite Ridge's credit facility borrowing base was increased to $325 million in November 2024, so it will still have a modest amount of liquidity at the end of 2025 if its borrowing base doesn't change.

I am assuming that Granite Ridge's borrowing base stays at $325 million, with production/reserve growth during 2025 offsetting the impact of lower oil prices. Granite Ridge's leverage target is under 1.25x, and it is projected to remain below that at the end of 2025. The projected cash burn after dividends is significant, though, and Granite Ridge would end 2025 with credit facility utilization of around 85% if nothing changes.

I had mentioned before that Granite Ridge would probably need to reduce growth plans at low-$60s oil, and believe there is a significant chance that its 2025 capex budget will be reduced a bit due to the oil strip being in the low-$60s for the remainder of the year.

Notes On Valuation

My previous estimate of Granite Ridge's value was $8.25 per share, based on strip prices for 2025 followed by $75 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after 2025.

I have revised my valuation calculations based on current strip prices until the end of 2026 now, followed by $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. For now, I have assumed that Granite Ridge doesn't make any major changes to its 2025 guidance.

I have lowered Granite Ridge's estimated value by $1.00 per share to $7.25 per share, reflecting increased near-term cash burn as well as a $5 decrease in long-term oil price expectations.

Granite Ridge's original plan was for its oil production to accelerate in the second half of 2025 with its second Delaware Basin Operated Partnership rig starting up in mid-February. I believe that Granite Ridge will continue with that for now, but I can see it deferring some completions if oil prices stay in the low-$60s.

Granite Ridge has also mentioned potentially adding another $60 million to $80 million in 2025 development capex (mostly benefitting 2026 results), but that additional spending would likely be off the table now given commodity prices.

Conclusion

Granite Ridge finished 2024 with Q4 2024 production increasing by 10% compared to Q3 2024 and ending up at 27,734 BOEPD. It expects some continued growth in 2025, with an average production of around 29,000 BOEPD.

This was 4% to 5% higher 2025 production than what I had modeled before, albeit with a $310 million capex budget compared to the $290 million modeled number.

With the weaker oil prices, I can see its production growth slowing later in 2025 and into 2026. However, I believe that Granite Ridge's production guidance for 2025 should remain largely intact. It may defer some activity later in the year to reduce its capex budget and cash burn for 2025.

I estimate Granite Ridge's value at $7.25 per share now, factoring in low-$60s oil for the rest of 2025 and 2026, and assuming long-term $70 WTI oil after that.

data.title: TSMC: Firing On All Cylinders After Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM), also known as TSMC, just released its fiscal second quarter 2025 earnings. The results mostly beat analyst estimates, with $25.53 billion in revenue (roughly in line with estimates) and $2.12 in earnings per share (beat by $0.06).

Going into the release, analysts were very concerned with TSMC’s commitments to President Donald Trump, which included pledges of $100 billion in investments into U.S. fabs. Trump wants TSMC to bring fabs to the United States in order to facilitate what he calls the “re-shoring” of American manufacturing. The U.S. has lost a considerable number of manufacturing jobs over the decades, and while unemployment remains relatively low, Trump still thinks that having manufacturing at home would be preferable to the status quo. Following this logic, he threatened Taiwan with tariffs and secured a $100 billion pledge from TSMC shortly afterward.

Taiwan Semiconductor stock started selling off after TSMC made that announcement. Possible reasons for the selloff include high CAPEX to build the new U.S. fabs; the perception that TSMC was coerced into doing something against its self-interest; and Taiwan’s loss of the “silicon shield” that it gained from housing the world’s most important semiconductor company. The last of those factors is perhaps the most important because it could encourage China to invade Taiwan (the U.S. response to an invasion would likely be weaker if the U.S. ceased being dependent on Taiwanese manufacturing).

So, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing got beaten down due to speculation that the U.S. fab investment might hurt its business. Indeed, some of the reasons for concern about the investment were valid. For example, the added CAPEX spend needed to build a new U.S. Fab would indeed be considerable, exceeding TSMC’s current cash and equivalents balance. The positive, assuming the project goes ahead, is the ability to serve the U.S. market more quickly than before.

So, investors are right to have some concerns about Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing. However, as we saw in the company’s first-quarter release, TSMC is doing well. The company is still highly profitable, growing and thriving. In addition, investors seem to have missed the positives from the big investments in U.S. fabs, which include U.S. market access, improved relationships with U.S. tech companies, and shorter delivery times.

When I last covered Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing, I rated the stock a hold, mainly because of its steep valuation. I think that rating was reasonable given the price at the time ($198). However, TSM stock is cheaper now, and the growth observed in Q2 was far greater than my expectations. Because TSM stock is down 20% since I last covered it, it is now a better buy than it was before. Owing to the combination of a strong earnings release and a cheaper stock price, I am upgrading TSMC to a Strong Buy.

Earnings Recap

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing’s Q1 release was a solid one, with earnings beating estimates and revenue roughly in line with estimates. Some highlights included:

  • $25.53B in revenue, up 40.1% year over year but down 3% sequentially (in line with estimates).

  • $11.12B in net income, up 60.3% (a beat).

  • $2.12 in diluted EPS, up 60.3% (a beat).

  • A 59% gross margin (in line with estimates).

  • Guidance for $25 to $25.8 billion in revenue and a 59% gross margin in Q2.

Overall, the results were quite satisfactory. The company’s growth streak continued, and the metrics it reported beat analyst estimates. In the commentary section of the release, management highlighted the success that the company was having with advanced 3nm and 5nm chips. The release stated that such advanced chips made up 67% of TSMC’s total revenue. That’s a positive because TSMC has a major edge in providing such chips: competitors can make chips with larger transistor sizes but aren’t as effective at making cutting-edge chips with smaller transistor sizes.

Speaking of which: on the earnings call that was held after TSMC’s release came out, management highlighted the progress it was making with 2nm transistors, which will power the world’s most advanced chips. So, there will be a lot to look forward to from TSMC in the year ahead.

Competitive Landscape

Perhaps the most impressive thing about TSMC is its competitive position. The company manufactures 60% of the world’s computer chips and 90% of the most advanced chips. It is making big progress with 2nm chips, which no other firm is capable of producing. TSMC’s 2nm chips are being described as the most advanced chips in the world, and TSMC will presumably have a monopoly on supplying them until another firm catches up with them. The production of 2nm chips is a competitive edge for TSMC. The company’s knowledge accumulated by serving clients with unique products (e.g. Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL)) and its lack of conflicts of interest with its customers are also major competitive edges. So, TSMC’s moat will likely remain intact in the near future.

Valuation

Having looked at TSMC’s recent results and competitive position, we can move on to valuing the stock.

First, let’s look at the multiples. According to Seeking Alpha Quant, TSM trades at 21 times earnings, 7.8 times sales, and 5.3 times book. This is a far cheaper valuation than most of the Magnificent 7 stocks–despite the fact that TSM has a better competitive position than many of them. So, TSM stock looks attractive from a relative valuation standpoint.

Next, we can look at TSM’s valuation from a discounted cash flow (DCF) perspective. Building a complete Excel model with a forecast is beyond the scope of this article. However, using a no-growth assumption, the company’s TTM earnings per ADR ($6.32) discounted at the 10-year treasury yield (4.3%) is worth $179. This implies a limited amount of upside; however, with management continuing to guide positive growth, it is likely that TSMC will see some growth in the years ahead. If we assume that the company can grow its earnings at just 2% for the foreseeable future, the fair value estimate rises to $333–considerable upside.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on TSMC’s earnings release is that it was more than satisfactory. The company beat earnings, yet its stock price barely budged after the release came out. Would I buy TSM stock today? In a heartbeat.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: History Is About To Repeat Itself: Another AMD 2022 Rally May Be Incoming
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock was down over 7% on Wednesday following the announcement of China chip restrictions, and I am maintaining the stock with a strong buy recommendation for long-term investors, as the current pullback presents the most compelling buy case of the current sell-off. I think AMD is a value pick, and I continue to think the stock is attractive at current levels as the negatives are, for the most part, priced in.

AMD's stock isn't priced for perfection, unlike Nvidia (NVDA), which leaves a lot of room for upside ahead. AMD stock is down around 17% since my last rating, and down over 26.9% year-to-date, against Nvidia down 22%, Intel (INTC) down around 4%, and the S&P 500 (SP500), also down over 10.3%, during the same period.

I initiated AMD with a buy in late March for long-term investors, precisely believing that AMD isn't an Nvidia (NVDA) number 2. Spoiler alert: My thesis now stands stronger.

Investment thesis:

My thesis on AMD is fairly simple: The company has a place in the market, regardless of Nvidia's fierce competition, as the appetite for AI grows. While Nvidia has long dominated the AI space, I think it's a mistake to cancel AMD as merely a distant second, as demand for diversified AI computing, including cost-efficient alternatives to Nvidia's tight CUDA stack, accelerates. AMD's GPUs have been gaining growing traction from hyperscalers like Meta (META), Oracle (ORCL), and Microsoft (MSFT), which to me suggests that AMD is no longer playing catch-up, but rather addressing the demand that Nvidia cannot possibly fulfill alone.

Take, for example, the last quarter of 2024, during which Nvidia lost around 8% of the discrete desktop GPU market due to constrained production capacity, while AMD gained around 7% and Intel gained around 1.2%, according to Jon Peddie Research. While Nvidia dominated the market with 82% market share, against AMD at 17% of shipments, there was never a question of who led the market.

So, while, yes, Nvidia leads the GPU market, AMD's role in addressing unmet needs is flying over investors' heads, and I fear that most aren't seeing the full picture. As demand surges to new highs, Nvidia's supply constraints are getting tighter, and AMD is filling that void. I remain optimistic about AMD's PC business as the company gains more market share from Intel. AMD was once a distant second to Intel in the PC market but has since snatched market share from Intel, which then had seemingly an unbreakable monopoly.

Price movement and valuation:

The stock is currently trading at its lowest valuation since 2022 and is approaching a level not seen since September of that same year.

AMD is setting up exactly as it did in 2022 before the stock surged from $65 to over $225. If AMD repeats its past behavior, a rally isn't all that far-fetched.

RSI is recovering from oversold levels at 21 and currently stands at 41.5, which is a bullish sign. Not to mention, volume bars spiking up on green days, which shows buying interest. I'd keep an eye on RSI, and if it crosses 50, that would signal even more strength and continuation. Resistance is currently at the $88-$99 range, and if AMD closes above this level with volume, there could be a breakout from the recent downward trend, so watch $90 as a key resistance level.

The stock is trading below all its EMAs at $88.2 per share. The stock is down over 57% from its March highs last year at around $207 per share.

Valuation was a selling point for me when I covered the stock in late March, and it remains so (even more now).

My thoughts on forward P/E are that every time it dips around the 20x level, a rebound happens. Forward P/E is currently standing at 19x, 53% below its 5-year average, and I spy a rebound in the near term. Not only is it attractive as a standalone number, but also when compared to Intel at 39.4x and Nvidia at 23.3x. I'm also watching the PEG ratio, which now stands at 0.66x, more attractive than it was the last time I covered the stock at 0.8x.

While all these signals show growing momentum for a breakout, I would tread carefully as the market volatility isn't to be taken lightly right now. Remember, don't try to time the market. If you're a long-term investor, use this pullback as a buying opportunity and add in bunches. But if you're a short-term investor or a trader looking to make some quick money, wait for stronger reversal signs and don't jump in just yet.

The restrictions: We saw this coming

Along with that, diverse exposure across AI, CPUs, gaming, and rising market share in PC chips make me optimistic about the company for the long term, "despite short-term pain". So don't say I didn't warn you. In my last article, I recognized the risks that U.S.-China trade tensions might pose, and sure enough, the worst has happened.

Just days after AMD was given the green light in terms of exporting its MI308 chips to China, with Trump pausing the China tariffs from hurting semiconductor chips, Tuesday brought a slap to the face. The White House had a sudden shift in heart on Sunday, and they hinted at putting new kinds of tariffs on semiconductor chips, and that's exactly what they did with the new ban on April 14th. The U.S. government doubled down and said the restrictions would remain in place indefinitely.

Now, the mass media is focusing on this new policy and the impact it will have on Nvidia, but way too much is being overlooked when it comes to AMD, if you ask me. The restrictions forced Nvidia to face a new charge of $5.5 billion for its first quarter of fiscal year 2026 ending April 27th, but AMD is also looking at a charge of $800 million, the company said in a Form 8-K:

The Export Control applies to the Company's MI308 products. The Company expects to apply for licenses, but there is no assurance that licenses will be granted. The Company expects that the Export Control may result in charges of up to approximately $800 million in inventory, purchase commitments, and related reserves.

AMD isn't unscathed here, at all, with the company making 24.2% of its revenue in China last year, 33.7% from the U.S., and the rest from Japan at 6.9%. And this isn't the company's first rodeo in restrictions. Nor is it Nvidia's.

AMD generated over $5 billion in data center AI revenues for FY2024, primarily driven by deployments of MI300X by Meta Platforms (META) and Microsoft. The company is looking for a significant hit to its revenue from China due to the recent restrictions, but I think that's now priced in with the 8% drop on Wednesday.

Food for thought: Some background on Nvidia's H20 chips

After curbs on the company's AI chips since 2022, the company introduced a chip designed to handle interface, called the H20, with Chinese tech companies Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), Alibaba (BABA), and ByteDance (BDNCE) ramping up orders since February after the DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) moment. All these companies together ordered at least $16 billion in order for H20 chips in the first three months of 2025 to get ahead of the potential U.S. ban. According to the report, Tencent installed H20 chips in a facility to train a large AI model and DeepSeek to train its V3 model, breaching U.S. export rules. The question is: will anyone come close to snatching Nvidia's market share in China? While Huawei tried to work on an alternative, its Ascend 910C, built on TSMC (TSM) and Super Micro (SMCI) 7nm, it's still lagging behind Nvidia's H100.

Now, all this is bad news, so why am I more buy-rated on AMD than before?

The company reports Q1 '25 on May 6 and is likely to recognize its charges in Q2 '25. While I don't think the company will miss on earnings, guidance is a hindrance here. Following the news of the restrictions, the stock dropped as much as 8% during the trading day on Wednesday, which makes me think the negatives are already priced in.

There's a running joke now that AMD stock always finds a way to drop, which I can't argue against. But when you're holding a stock for the long term, short-term hiccups sound like an opportunity; a gift that keeps on giving.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SL Green: Management Reiterates Guidance And Hints At An Update With An Upside Bias
data.description:
During the conference call for its just-released Q1 earnings, SL Green Realty Corp. (NYSE:SLG) management updated investors that its original guidance was definitely still intact. In fact, management was above guidance even though the occupancy percentage declined. The goal to increase the occupancy was further reinforced. There is an expectation that guidance will be revised during the next conference call with an upwards bias (right now).

Supposedly, SL Green has a long history of taking advantage of periods of uncertainty. That is exactly the plan right now. The weaker dollar is viewed as a huge plus, while management believes that they are nimble enough to take advantage of the tumult in the debt market. In short, there is a plan in place to take advantage of the uncertainty now moving through the business.

FFO

The conference call noted that Funds From Operations, or FFO, came in well above the first quarter plan.

In the previous year, there was a gain of more than $2 per share that is affecting the comparison. After that, both the current quarter and the previous quarter have some small one-time adjustments that really do not affect the fact that FFO showed good growth over the previous year from continuing business operations.

There has been a lot of leasing activity, and the conference call reported a fair amount of buyer interest where that was needed. This company has long had periodic gains and losses as part of the results. There is an expectation that will continue.

Last Article

As the last article and several articles noted before it, management far outperformed market expectations, which resulted in this stock being one of the top office REIT performers in the industry. While many office REIT's I follow have struggled, this company was buying and selling properties as if there was no downturn.

This is probably attributable to the New York City market's outperformance in general compared to many other locations. The city has a wide variety of businesses represented. That diversification has allowed the city to have a stronger performance than is the case elsewhere in the nation.

Now it likely helps that this company has some of the best located properties in that city. As a result, future outperformance is likely. It is also likely that if this company does have a "hiccup" or worse, much of the country will be in far worse shape.

The follow-up to this last article was a report that business is still booming for SL Green in New York City. There were plenty of "what-if" questions. But the answer was the same as last year. Given the situation, the correct price (whether as a buyer or as a seller) will get the transaction done.

The balance sheet on the supplemental reports shows signs of growing again. This reverses the liquidation process that led to a lot of debt payments in the previous fiscal year. It is also a sign of management's optimism.

Note that in the last fiscal year, management began buying properties because it was noted that returns during a recovery are often phenomenal.

Like me, there is a belief that what is going on now will be replaced by something constructive, predictable, and permanent. The overall outlook between the two quarters remains optimistic.

Rental Or Lease Decline

Shown below is one of the key market results over several quarters.

There was some concern about the overall rental (or leasing) rate decline reported in the first quarter in comparison to the same quarter for the previous fiscal year.

This company is a relatively small player in a large market. Therefore, quarterly rates reported depend largely upon the activity that was reportable. So, the rental rate can vary quite a bit based upon location differences. It is not a sign of trouble unless a negative comparison persists for a while.

The managed rental properties are located in different parts of New York City. It is entirely possible that those different places command different rates. It is also very possible that those rates are not comparable to the previous quarter, as the activity could well have centered in a different location.

The answer to questions during the conference call is that rates have held steady, combined with a review of the pipeline of potential leases and rentals (and maybe some sales). There is no indication of slowing activity (which is the key concern).

Last Year

The biggest issue that came up during the question-and-answer session was all the gains (and some losses) reported last year. This company is known for probably more gains than is typical of the usual office REIT. That makes analysis difficult.

Already in the first quarter, there was a gain in the previous year that had no counterpart in the current fiscal year. That is an unfortunate feature of gains. It is especially true when a smaller company like this one "regularly" to some extent, benefits from those gains over time.

Much of the time the investor ends up depending upon the representations of management about the progress the company is making because those periodic gains happen unpredictably. Here, that unpredictable nature is part of the business.

There are continuing operations that are also part of the story. There is also a new "asset lite" strategy that seems to be doing well since its initial announcement. The challenge for Mr. Market is gauging the progress when a perfectly normal quarter like this one is compared to one with other items in it.

That may, at times affect the stock price valuation. Going against the downside, was the achievement of much of the fiscal year 2024 guidance at a time when Mr. Market definitely did not expect it. That included some very material sales at good prices that "were never going to happen" according to the market. That gives management some credence that they know what they are doing this fiscal year.

Summary

SL Green far outperformed the industry in fiscal year 2024. That led to a fantastic stock price performance as management far exceeded market expectations.

As is shown above, Mr. Market is again beginning to doubt management during yet another period of uncertainty. However, management did note that the weaker dollar is a friend of real estate. This is especially true in a market like New York City, where there is international interest in the real estate.

For investors, the current yield is getting to the historical high side, even though that yield has yet to recover to pre-covid levels. This reinforces the strong buy case.

The fact that management maintains the optimism to guide to an expected 2025 revision upward makes the strong buy consideration even more solid.

New York City has so far proven to be a far stronger office market than has been the case in many places in the United States. There is no reason that should not continue. Personally, I would consider holding this until the story changes materially, or the stock becomes far overpriced.

Risks

The current tariff threat and a potential trade war threat are assumed to be negotiating positions that will be replaced with something constructive, predictable, well communicated, and permanent. There is no assurance that will happen.

Management's current assessments of the effects on major conditions like the debt market volatility and weaker dollar could prove to be overly optimistic. Every once in a while, Mr. Market is right about the worst possible outcome. Given the past performance of management, I do like the odds that management will again outperform expectations. However, there are no guarantees in the investment business.

A loss of key personnel could prove to be a major issue that would set back the future of the company.

data.title: Granite Construction: Continued Growth And Valuation Justify Optimism
data.description:
When the market is behaving in a volatile way, especially when that volatility brings significant downside, it can be unnerving. It's never fun to lose money. Many investors make the mistake of cashing out entirely and waiting for the crisis to end. But the problem with this is that timing the market is more speculation than it is anything else. The best thing to do during difficult times is to allocate your capital toward high-quality companies that are trading at attractive levels. A good example of this can be seen by looking at Granite Construction (NYSE:GVA).

In my last article about Granite Construction, published in January of this year, I reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate. This was based on strong financial performance leading up to that point and how attractively priced shares were. I did acknowledge that the enterprise was getting to the point where it was almost deserving of a downgrade to a ‘hold’. But I ended up not pulling the trigger at that time. From that time through today, the stock is down 12.5%. That's worse than the 11% drop experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time. However, holding through the ups and downs has, in the past, proven to be rewarding for investors in this name. From the time I originally rated the company a ‘buy’ in March 2023, shares are up an astounding 93.1%. That's approximately triple the 31.6% upside achieved by the broader market. With the stock now looking cheaper than it was previously, I do believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate makes sense, especially given management's expectations for 2025.

Buying the Dip Could Make Sense

In case you aren't familiar with Granite Construction, the company operates as an infrastructure solutions business that caters to both public and private clients across the US. In the public sector, it emphasizes infrastructure projects such as streets, highways, mass transit facilities, airports, bridges, dams, and more. And on the private side of things, it provides customers with services such as site preparation, mining services, and infrastructure services for a wide array of clients such as those that own railways, those engaged in residential development, those involved in energy development, and more.

As the image above illustrates, the enterprise focuses on most parts of the US. Through its Construction segment, the company helps with things such as pavement preservation, the construction and rehabilitation of roads, and more. And through its Materials segment, the company produces aggregates, asphalt concrete, liquid asphalt, and recycled materials. The facilities that the business has dedicated to these initiatives are located mostly in California, Washington, and Mississippi. But that doesn't stop the firm from providing its services to 27 other States and the US territory of Guam. All told, the company ended 2024 with 40 asphalt crushing plants. That compares to the 35 reported one year earlier. It kept the number of asphalt concrete plants flat at 59. The number of cement concrete batch plants jumped from 6 to 10. Meanwhile, it maintained the 4 asphalt rubber plants and the 6 lime slurry plants that it ended 2023 with.

When it comes to the 2024 fiscal year, the business did quite well for itself and its investors. Revenue, for instance, totaled $4.01 billion. That's an increase of 14.2% compared to the $3.51 billion generated just one year earlier. The Materials segment for the company grew the fastest, shooting up from $516.9 million to $592.3 million. That's an increase of 14.6%. According to management, this was driven mostly by acquisitions that the company had made, though it did also benefit from higher asphalt and aggregates sales prices.

Speaking of acquisitions, during the year, Granite Construction made one acquisition. This was of Dickerson & Bowen, an aggregate, asphalt, and highway construction company that caters to customers in central and southern Mississippi. That cost shareholders $125.5 million. However, it should bring in around $55.2 million in annualized revenue for the company. In 2023, Granite Construction made two other acquisitions. In November of that year, it acquired LRC/MSG in exchange for $278 million. That operation focuses on asphalt paving and asphalt and aggregates production. Then, also that year, in April to be precise, Granite Construction purchased Coast Mountain Resources, a construction aggregate producer based out of British Columbia, Canada. However, management did not mention how much this cost and instead stated that it did not have a material impact on the company's financial results.

The firm’s Construction segment also saw growth, with revenue climbing 14.1% from $2.99 billion to $3.42 billion. Management claimed that this was because of a rise in CAP (Committed and Awarded Projects) at the start of 2024. In the image above, you can see that the company saw $5.62 billion worth of CAP in the third quarter of 2024. That did eventually drop to $5.30 billion in the final quarter of last year. That, consequently, was down from the $5.55 billion achieved in the final quarter of 2023. Other contributors behind this sales increase included more favorable weather conditions earlier in 2024 and the $114.7 million that the company got because of the timing of two of the three acquisitions I mentioned already.

With revenue rising, profits and cash flows for the company jumped as well. Net income nearly tripled from $43.6 million to $126.3 million. The rise in revenue for the company, as well as a reduction in ‘other costs’ from $50.2 million to $39.9 million, certainly helped. However, the company also enjoyed an increase in the gross profit margin for its Construction segment from 10.9% to 14.4%. Operating efficiencies caused by the additional revenue played a big role here. However, management also said that inexperienced improved project execution across its project portfolio. Other profitability metrics performed well without exception. Adjusted net profits jumped from $147.4 million to $214 million. Operating cash flow expanded from $183.7 million to $456.3 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a rise from $182.6 million to $315.4 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $279.4 million to $401.7 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the final quarter of 2024 compared to the same time in 2023. The growth in revenue, profits, and all cash flow figures, are all indicative of the growth trend for the company continuing. What's more, management seems optimistic about 2025. They expect, for instance, revenue for this year to be between $4.2 billion and $4.4 billion. At the midpoint, that would be 7.3% above the $4.01 billion reported for 2024. Management anticipates an EBITDA margin of between 11% and 12%. The midpoint there would translate to EBITDA of $494.5 million. Should this come to fruition, it would likely mean a rise in adjusted net profits to $263.4 million and an increase in adjusted operating cash flow to $388.3 million.

If we use the historical results from 2024 and the estimates for 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. On an absolute basis, shares are attractive to me. This is especially true relative to cash flows. Compared to other similar firms, the stock is a bit on the cheap side of the spectrum. On a price to earnings basis, only one of the five companies I compared it to in the table below ended up being cheaper than it is. This number rises to two of the five when using the EV to EBITDA approach and rises further to three of the five on a price to operating cash flow basis.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Granite Construction 15.9 10.8 9.0
MYR Group (MYRG) 61.2 21.5 15.9
Construction Partners (ROAD) 74.5 22.2 26.4
Primoris Services (PRIM) 17.4 6.2 8.1
Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) 16.3 8.5 8.5
Ameresco (AMRC) 9.5 4.6 10.7

It will be interesting when management eventually comes out with data for the first quarter of 2025. This is because there really wasn't much data at the time that final quarter results for 2024 came out regarding the tariff situation and the impact it could have on the business. Management did warn in their annual report that tariffs, as well as elevated interest rates, supply chain issues, wars and geopolitical tensions, and other factors, could negatively affect the enterprise. Even so, the business still said that organic revenue growth through the year 2027 should be between 6% and 8% per annum. The company also sees its EBITDA margin climbing to between 12% and 14%. Add on top of this the benefit that could be had by continued bolt-on acquisitions, as well as other, larger, transactions, and it's not unthinkable that the company could grow revenue, profits, and cash flows, quite a bit between now and the end of 2027.

One thing that certainly helps the company is that most of its operations center on the public sector as opposed to the private sector. Historically speaking, the public sector has fared better during difficult times. As of the end of 2024, $4.12 billion, or 77.8%, of its CAP involved public projects. That left only $1.18 billion for the private sector. As I mentioned at the start of this article, the company does focus a great deal on operations in California. And it just so happens that Caltrans (California Department of Transportation) is a project behemoth. For the 2024/2025 operating year, Caltrans allocated $9.02 billion, inclusive of $2.93 billion of local assistance, to its expenditure budget. And for the next year, it's expected to be even higher at $9.84 billion. This should certainly help the enterprise, even if broader economic conditions worsen.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I believe that Granite Construction remains an attractive prospect. The fact that shares have fallen since my last article means that investors have an opportunity to get in at more attractive prices than they did previously. I do think that there is definitely some uncertainty on the economic front. But given how cheap the stock currently is and how well management has been doing the last couple of years and expects to continue doing for the foreseeable future, I believe that Granite Construction deserves a ‘buy’ rating.

data.title: Nurix Therapeutics: IRAK4 GS-6971 Isn't Only Protein Degrader With Big Potential
data.description:
Nurix Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:NRIX) just announced today that it had received IND clearance from the FDA for its BTK degrader GS-6791, which is being developed to target inflammatory disorders. In essence, going after such disorders like rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and Atopic Dermatitis (AD). The thing to note is that this program is being done in collaboration with Gilead Sciences (GILD) and it is the one responsible for continuing development of this drug. However, because IND clearance was granted from the FDA to advance a phase 1 study using GS-6791 for this program, Nurix received a $5 million milestone payment under the collaboration agreement established between both companies. Things have been coming along well since this partnership was first established back in 2019.

That's because this small-cap biotech has stated that it earned approximately $135 million under this specific collaboration agreement. Along with the fact that it will be eligible to earn up to $420 in development, regulatory, and commercial milestone payments to develop the use of this IRAK4 molecule degrader against inflammatory disorders. The beauty of this biotech is that this isn't the only collaboration agreement that it has done thus far. It also has partnerships to develop protein degraders with Pfizer (PFE) and Sanofi (SNY). Speaking of Sanofi, Nurix is working with this big pharma to develop a STAT6 protein degrader to target TH2-mediated inflammatory disorders, such as Asthma, Atopic Dermatitis, and Allergies. The drug that this company is working on, its wholly owned one, would be Bexobrutideg (NX-5948).

This oral brain-penetrant small molecule degrader of BTK is currently being tested in a phase 1b study to treat patients with relapsed/refractory B-Cell malignancies. Why should investors care about this program? It is because the company is in the process of starting a host of global registration studies for this program, with the first target of choice being the treatment of patients with chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL).

A good thing here is that BTK targeting is not only applicable to targeting hematological malignancies but can also be used to target patients with inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. With that said, the goal is to open up cohorts in the phase 1b study to move towards this area as well.

GS-6791 Provides Another Degrader Target Beyond That Of BTK

As I noted above, the company has full rights to its BTK degrader Bexobrutideg (NX-5948), and this is being explored in a phase 1b study to target relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies. Plus, the goal is for it to also test this drug against inflammatory and autoimmune disorders. Protein degradation of BTK is ideal because it is a known target in the oncology space. Nurix believes that BTK degradation may end up being superior to that of BTK inhibition.

While this remains to be seen, the company has another degrader program in its pipeline that it is developing in collaboration with Gilead Sciences. This other protein degrader is known as GS-6791 (NX-0479) and as I stated above, IND clearance was given so that Gilead could initiate a phase 1 program for this candidate. Specifically, it is expected to initiate a phase 1 single-ascending dose(SAD) and multiple-ascending dose (MAD) study for inflammatory disorders. The two indications of choice for this program are expected to be that of rheumatoid arthritis and Atopic Dermatitis. Before going over the preclinical data that got GS-6791 to be given a green light for phase 1 testing, plus any possible catalysts to be released from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what these disorders are and what the possible market opportunity for each could end up being.

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a type of autoimmune disorder whereby the joints of the body are greatly affected. However, this can also affect other parts of the body as well, such as the heart, lungs, and skin. Like all other autoimmune disorders, the immune system mistakenly attacks the person's body, causing inflammation. With RA, though, there the lining of the joints is damaged and the bones underneath them are eaten away as well. The global rheumatoid arthritis drugs market is expected to reach $53.1 billion by 2031. The hope is that the IRAK4 degrader GS-6791 is going to be able to help treat these patients in an effective manner.

The other indication being targeted is Atopic Dermatitis (AD). This is an autoimmune disorder as well, which is characterized by the formation of dry, itchy, and inflamed skin. This inflammatory disorder is also known as Eczema and typically affects younger people. On the flip side, it can also occur at any age as well. This is a devastating disorder and the reason why is because it is chronic by nature. Besides the issue with the skin, in severe cases, it could actually get worse. This would be in terms of AD causing other problems to arise like obtaining a fever or asthma. The global Atopic Dermatitis market is expected to grow to $19.49 billion by the end of 2034. This is another huge blockbuster market opportunity for both Nurix and its partner Gilead Sciences.

As I stated at the beginning of this article, the IND of GS-6791 was cleared by the FDA today. This now gives Gilead Sciences the ability to initiate the expected phase 1 program for it. This leads the way to have it begin a phase 1 SAD and MAD study in healthy volunteers in Q2 of 2025. With this IND being given clearance, Nurix received a $5 million payment from its partner. To date, it has received a total of $135 million because of this collaboration agreement. If things continue to go well with respect to further progress being achieved in the phase 1 study and beyond, then it would also be eligible to earn up to $420 million in development, regulatory, and commercial milestones because of it.

The collaboration agreement itself was initiated back in 2019 between both of these companies. It was generated so that up to a total of 5 protein degraders could be developed under this program to target patients with cancer and other challenging disorders. Here is where the agreement that was established gets a bit more interesting. Upon completion of a phase 1 study (for the 5 molecule degraders being developed), Nurix has the option to co-develop and co-detail up to two targets. Why is the targeting of IRAK4? That's because this protein signaling molecule plays a critical role in the Toll-like receptor (TLR) and Interleukin-1 family (IL-1R) signaling pathways. A protein degrader like GS-6791 is highly ideal to target this pathway because of a scaffold and kinase activity in place. Another reason why a protein degrader might be appropriate here is because an inhibitor can't do much against the IRAK4 protein.

That is, inhibition of this protein signaling target is not enough to establish full targeting efficacy. This is why Nurix, along with its partner Gilead, believe that they can adequately go after IRAK4 with this protein degrader. The most promising aspect of GS-6791 and all other degraders being developed in the pipeline is that they act like gene therapies. That is protein degradation achieves the very same function of silencing genes.

Why was this drug further developed? It is because of positive findings that were achieved in pre-clinical testing. With in-vitro testing, it was noted that not only was potent IRAK4 degradation achieved, but there was the suppression of cytokines as well. The point here is that protein degradation with GS-6791 can likely target the full pathway of TLR/IL-1R (IL-14) where kinase inhibitors can't. This was shown in pre-clinical testing in an arthritis model. The hope is that this protein degrader continues to do well because then that would mean that Nurix is going to be in good shape for the expected $420 million in milestone payments.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Nurix Therapeutics had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $549.7 million as of February 28th 2025. An important thing to note is that this doesn't include approximately $7 million of cash earned from milestone payments earned during the three months ending this particular Q1 of 2025 earnings. Along with a $15 million license extension payment received post-quarter end. Despite all these partnerships in place, the company will still need to find ways to raise cash to operate. This isn't just a theory, but what is stated in its 10-Q SEC Filing, which is that it believes that it has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Nurix Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the IND clearance given for GS-6791 for the treatment of patients with inflammatory disorders. A phase 1 study to test this IRAK4 protein degrader in healthy volunteers is expected to start soon enough. The risk here is that even though this molecule was shown to work well in in-vitro and Non-human primate testing, it doesn't mean it will work when it is tested in humans in the first study. If desired efficacy is not achieved with GS-6791, not only would it cause the stock price to slide, but Gilead could end up deciding to terminate its partnership agreement.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of Bexobrutideg for the treatment of patients with hematological malignancies and inflammatory disorders. The goal of the company is to evaluate this drug in a study targeting patients with Chronic Lymphocytic leukemia (CLL). The risk here is that even if such a phase 1 study is initiated, there is no assurance that this protein degrader will perform well. Secondly, it is believed that BTK degradation can better counter resistance observed with other drugs and scaffolds that are in place. The risk here is that there is no assurance that Bexobrutideg is going to be able to counter these specific issues observed with other BTK inhibitors or any other drug class.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that it is in. The reason why is that it doesn't have an extended cash runway at all. As a matter of fact, it believes that it only has enough cash to fund its business for at least the next 12 months. This means that it is going to have to find a way to raise funds using some type of financial tool at its disposal in the next several months.

Conclusion

Nurix Therapeutics has done well in developing the protein degraders in its pipeline. The most recent news was that it was able to obtain a $5 million milestone payment from its partner Gilead Sciences, because of the FDA giving IND clearance for a phase 1 study to be initiated. Just on this partnership alone, in developing GS-6791 for the treatment of patients with inflammatory disorders, it is eligible to earn up to $420 million in development, regulatory and commercial milestones. This isn't the only collaboration agreement it has made in terms of the development of protein degraders from its pipeline. Just last year, it extended its collaboration agreement with this big pharma to develop a STAT-6 degrader to go after Type 2 inflammatory diseases.

The first candidate to be nominated for this program is expected to happen within the first year of this agreement being signed. The whole premise of why this biotech has potential is because of its BTK protein degraders and beyond. The reason is that BTK degraders have many advantages over BTK inhibitors. For instance, protein degraders can: Overcome treatment-emergent resistance mutations, the ability to counter BTK scaffolding, can target B-Cell malignancies in a superior fashion, and target unmet medical needs in autoimmune and inflammatory disorders that have not yet been sought out. With all of this said, Bexobrutideg can potentially achieve superior BTK targeting, which means that Nurix could capture a huge chunk of the BTK market.

According to the company's Corporate Presentation Slide #8, the BTK inhibitors market exceeded $10 billion in 2024. Next-generation BTK inhibitors are taking market share away from them. One such BTK inhibitor that is having its market share being taken away is IMBRUVICA (ibrutinib). What's the problem here? Well, even the next-generation BTK inhibitors share vulnerabilities, with mutations being resistant to them. This is where Bexobrutideg might shine, in that it can overcome such resistance. Of course, this has to be proven in human testing first. With the continued advancement of the GS-6971 selective oral IRAK4 degrader pressing on towards phase 1 testing, plus the potential to establish a foothold in the BTK market with Bexobrutideg, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Stellantis Faces Significant Exposure To Tariffs
data.description:
Stellantis N.V. (NYSE:STLA) released a challenging Q1 2025 vehicle delivery report on April 11, 2025, showing aggregate vehicle deliveries declining by -9% with a -20% decline in both North America and APAC. Despite management’s upbeat tone in their FY 2024 earnings call, I believe that the shift in trade policy, particularly as it relates to the automotive industry, may create a challenging market environment for Stellantis in the near-term. Given that the 25% automotive tariffs are carved out from the broader 10% tariff policy, these tariffs may be stickier as negotiations persist. As a result, I am recommending STLA with a Sell rating with a price target of $6.31/share at 1.32x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

All financial figures are denominated in USD.

Stellantis Operations

Stellantis has been gradually losing market share in the U.S., declining from nearly 11% in 2022 to 8% in 2024. Net sales in North America declined by -31% in FY 2024 when compared to the previous fiscal year, posing a major challenging for Stellantis given North America’s 40% share of global revenue for the firm. Accordingly, the North American market accounts for 31% of global profitability.

Stellantis is proactively adjusting operations as a result of the 25% automotive tariffs imposed on Canadian imports into the U.S. This includes pausing production at two factories located in Canada and Mexico as well as laying off 900 workers across 5 U.S. stamping, casting, and transmission plants. The tariff regime has the potential to leave Stellantis exposed to further operational decline, as the automaker imports roughly 35% of the vehicles sold in the U.S.

In addition to this, management made certain adjustments to the supply chain, delivering parts and components from the manufacturers that may be exposed to tariffs. Management also worked with Stellantis’s dealerships to ensure adequate inventory was delivered to meet their needs before tariffs were enacted. Accordingly, dealerships have 70–80 days of units onsite that may be impacted by the 25% automotive tariff on imports from Canada and Mexico.

Stellantis may be more exposed to tariffs when compared to other automakers, given the firm’s challenging sales environment. Prior to the tariff imposition, Stellantis reported its Q1 2025 vehicle deliveries with a company-wide -9% decline in unit volume sales. North America and APAC faced the largest declines, both falling -20% when compared to the previous year’s quarter. Maserati, Stellantis’s luxury brand, declined by -48% in unit volume sales. What’s somewhat surprising is that dealership inventory levels in North America remained stable with a 20% year-over-year inventory build, primarily in vehicles that would have been impacted by tariffs.

One interesting factor management mentioned during the Q1 2025 vehicle deliveries update call was that the firm issued dealership incentive pricing for vehicles in the U.S., discounting vehicles from inventory. Though management didn’t discern on their thought process behind implementing these discounts, I believe they could be the result of a number of factors, including making room for new inventory, providing a cushion for U.S. consumers facing inflationary pressures on day-to-day cost of living items, or as a last-ditch effort to boost sales.

Unit volumes were in part impacted by lower production following the curtailment of certain production in 2h24. The firm also transitioned production to the updated 2025 heavy-duty Ram 2500 & 3500 models in Q1 2025, leading to a temporary decline in production rates.

In order to get ahead of the tariffs, management had made plans in early 2025 to bolster its U.S. manufacturing footprint by restarting its Belvidere, Il, plant to produce its new midsize pickup, which is currently being manufactured in Mexico. Stellantis is also investing in its operations in Toledo, Ohio and Kokomo, Indiana, to bring more production to the U.S.

Stellantis has also been losing its footing in the European markets as well, declining from 19.7% in 2022 down to 17% in 2024. This may be in part the result of the production transition to the new Fiat Grande Panda, Citroën C3 Aircross, and the Opel Frontera.

The European automotive market has been relatively mixed so far in 2025, following a modest decline in sales in 2024. GlobalData is forecasting vehicle sales in the region to decline to 11.5mm vehicles in 2025, down from 11.55mm in the previous year. The trend, in part, is the result of the current tariff regime between European countries and the U.S., as well as inflationary pressures across the Bloc. According to GlobalData, March 2025 vehicle sales have been relatively robust when compared to the previous year’s period, likely the result of consumers purchasing vehicles before trade tariffs took effect on vehicle pricing. Core markets for Stellantis, including Germany and France, experienced significant declines in automotive sales while Spain, Italy, and the U.K. realized robust growth.

Stellantis Financial Position

In Q4 2024, management guided a stronger 1h25 when compared to the previous year’s period. Given the recent release of Q1 2025 unit shipments and the recently imposed 25% tariffs for automotive imports, I suspect guidance may have been overzealous despite being provided at the end of February 2025. So far, total unit shipments are down -11% in Q1 2025 when compared to the previous year’s quarter, suggesting either Stellantis will have a blockbuster Q2 2025 or have to adjust its expectations for the remainder of eFY25.

Given the potential headwinds from weakening consumer sentiment, discretionary spending may be widely selective as consumers grapple with day-to-day inflationary pressures like food and shelter. Despite my presumptions, consumer spending on vehicles and parts remained strong in February 2025, likely the result of consumers getting ahead of the potential price increases that may result from tariffs.

In terms of growth, Stellantis may realize an upswing as a result of the full ramp-up of certain launches like the Smart Car in the European market while North American sales may benefit from the return of the Charger, the Ramcharger, and the midsize Jeep SUV.

For eFY25, I’m forecasting Stellantis to generate $138b in net revenue, with a diluted EPS of $1.49/share. My rationale behind my forecast is that Stellantis may face certain challenges relating to the automotive tariffs, potentially leading to certain margin compression. In addition to this, I’m expecting that the dealership sales prices will push down the average selling prices for vehicles in North America following a year of significant pricing decline in 2024.

Risks Related to Stellantis

Bull Case

Stellantis is shifting its business model in an attempt to improve selling prices of vehicles by limiting dealership inventories. With the new lineup of vehicles in the U.S. and Europe, Stellantis may return to growth as production volumes ramp up.

Bear Case

The 25% automotive tariff is carved out from the broader tariff policy that is currently being negotiated between the U.S. and other countries. The automotive market faces significant tariff risks that may result in international trade being rerouted and nearshored to the U.S. Tariffs could potentially upend management’s goal of transitioning Stellantis back to growth, potentially pitting market share and average selling prices against each other.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Stellantis has a relatively robust shareholder return policy, targeting 25-30% of net income to be returned through dividends and share repurchases.

STLA shares currently trade at a significant discount to its peers of 11.35x EV/EBITDA on an unadjusted basis. This discount may be justified, especially in today’s market environment, as Stellantis may be more exposed to tariffs than its competitors.

Given the market risks, I am recommending STLA with a Sell rating with a price target of $6.31/share at 1.32x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Owens & Minor: A Painful Transformation
data.description:
In September of last year, I believed that Owens & Minor, Inc. (NYSE:OMI) was too aggressive on the dealmaking front. This came after the company has pursued many large deals in the past, and to be very kind, it has a mixed track record with such endeavors.

Substantial debt employed as well as a soft operating performance weighed on the shares, as a CFO move did not instill confidence given this background. With the company announcing another deal at the time, I found it very easy to shy away, given the arguments mentioned above. Now, with earnings coming on May 2nd, let's take another look.

A Medical Business With A Troubled Background

Owens & Minor for a long time was somewhat of a boring and predictable medical distribution business. That was the case in the 2010s when shares were glued around the $30 mark.

Following the purchase of Halyard Health's surgical and infection business, the company suffered from a real debt overhang and soft performance as a $30 stock fell to just $3 pre-pandemic. The irony was that Covid-19 provided a lifeline to the business, as shares recovered to the $50 mark in its aftermath, with the business enjoying a real boom. This provided the company with a window of opportunity to tap equity markets in order to reduce leverage.

After this narrow escape, I was surprised that Owens & Minor announced a $1.6 billion deal for sleep apnea business Apria in 2022, adding just 12% to its $10 billion (lower-margin) sales base. Pro forma EBITDA was seen around $700 million on over $11 billion in sales, yet pro forma leverage would jump to 3.5 times EBITDA, rather high, certainly as the core business was returning to normal circumstances after reporting peak profits during the pandemic.

In the end, 2023 sales normalized to $10.3 billion, with EBITDA down to $525 million. While earnings fell from $3 and change to just over $1 per share (albeit a highly adjusted number) leverage ratios were stable around 3.5 times EBITDA amidst deleveraging efforts, with net debt down to $1.85 billion.

The company guided for 2024 sales to advance modestly to $10.7 billion, with earnings seen up modestly to $1.55 per share. The market was quite skeptical on this, as a $14 stock in the summer of last year, represented just $1.1 billion in equity valuation, with shares trading at high single-digits earnings multiple.

That situation was tricky enough already, amidst CFO turnover, as the company announced a $1.32 billion deal to acquire Rotech, a home medical equipment provider, in a deal set to add $750 million in sales and $200 million in EBITDA.

While these looked like reasonable multiples, it was part of a strategy of the company to move away from lower margin to higher margin activities. Nonetheless, I was very skeptical. Pro forma net debt was seen at $3.2 billion, with pro forma EBITDA seen at around $800 million, for a leverage ratio of around 4 times.

More dealmaking was not the solution in my eyes, better execution and performance were.

Shares Are Plunging

Since the fall of last year, shares have come under continued pressure, with shares now trading at just $6 and change. After reporting non-eventful third quarter results in November, the company pre-announced its fourth-quarter results early in February.

Preliminary full-year sales were seen at $10.67-$10.70 billion, with adjusted EBITDA seen at $520-$525 million, adjusted earnings at $1.50-$1.53 per share, and net debt around $1.81 billion, ahead of Rotech of course (which is anticipated to close in the first half of this year). At the same time, a large GAAP loss was reported amidst a $310 million goodwill impairment related to the purchase Apria. All this caused a massive sell-off in the shares to high single digits.

Definitive results were reported by year-end, with the final results seen in line with the preliminary results. The company guided for 2025 sales at around $11.0 billion (plus or minus $150 million), EBITDA at $575 million (plus or minus $15 million), with adjusted earnings seen between $1.60-$1.85 per share.

This, of course, is the guidance on an organic basis, excluding the pending Rotech deal, as it furthermore excludes a potential transaction involving the sale of the Products & Healthcare Services segment.

This is and was the backbone of the business, which generated $8.0 billion in sales in 2024, yet posted segment operating earnings of a mere $53 million. Of course, any sales price is highly uncertain, but with this deal, the company would make a full transformation to higher value-added business lines.

By now, the 77 million shares of the business value equity of the business at just half a billion at $6 and change. This excludes a current $1.8 billion net debt load (pre-Rotech) as the big question is how much money will be fetched for the sale of the distribution activities.

What Now?

Amidst the many moving parts, I am disappointed with the shares for the reasons mentioned above. A sale of the Products & Healthcare Services segment could change this thesis a great deal, although the proceeds of such a potential transaction are highly uncertain, of course.

The uncertainty is once more confirmed by recent debt pricing actions, as the company issued $1 billion in notes which carry a 10% coupon, as using such expensive money to acquire a business almost always seems like a very bad idea.

Not convinced about the actions, including the prices involved with the transaction, I am leaning cautious, but I'm very interested in watching the shares from here. This is certainly the case if favorable pricing is potentially obtained for the Products & Healthcare Service business.

data.title: Bullish On Mexico Fund Though NAV Discount Is Likely To Persist
data.description:
I'm bullish on Mexico in both the short-term and long-term due to its strategic positioning. Mexico has been the United States' largest source of imported goods since 2023 when it overtook China.

That trade relationship deepened during the first Trump Administration, when Mexico and Canada struck a new deal with the United States, updating the old NAFTA to the new USMCA trade agreement. With this modernized regulatory framework, executives felt more comfortable deploying capital to build new factories in Mexico. The pandemic accelerated this nearshoring trend as companies sought to shorten supply lines, build additional redundancy into their logistics and reduce reliance on overseas production facilities.

This trend toward growing Mexican manufacturing appeared to be at risk over the past six months, however. Investors sold Mexico off sharply both due to the election of Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum last year, and with President Trump's return to power in the United States. There was a lot of back and forth around potential tariffs on Canada and Mexico, and we saw a meaningful decline in both the Mexican Peso and its associated equities over the past year.

In January, however, I suggested that Mexican stocks should start rallying once Trump was inaugurated and investors saw that Trump's rhetoric was worse than his actual policies as it pertains to Mexico. So far, so good on that front:

Over the past three months, Mexican stocks are up about 12%. That far outpaces emerging markets, which are flat, and the total world stock index and S&P 500, both of which are down considerably over the same period.

Needless to say, I remain bullish on Mexico as a whole. It appears to be a meaningful winner from the current realignment in trade. While the tariffs situation remains in flux, it appears that Canada and Mexico should continue to see zero percent tariffs on most of their goods which are USMCA-compliant. Meanwhile, China, the largest competitor in terms of U.S. imports, is facing an overwhelmingly high tariff burden as things stand now.

If the current state of play holds, Mexico should be expected to gain significant market share in manufacturing over the next few years. This should lead to rising employment, wages, and thus consumer spending within Mexico over time. That should prove to be a strong tailwind for Mexican equities. I also would expect the Mexican Peso to strengthen at least modestly compared to the U.S. Dollar as investors have been selling off U.S. assets recently while Mexico should see significant capital inflows to support further manufacturing and logistics investments.

So, I am bullish on Mexico. But, how about the Mexico Fund, Inc (NYSE:MXF)? Is this closed-end fund ("CEF") an attractive way to take part in the continuing surge in Mexico's manufacturing sector and broader economic opportunity?

Mexico Fund Versus The Mexico ETF

A central question when considering a closed-end fund for a given asset class is whether it can outperform an ETF that offers the same asset class exposure. In the case of the Mexico Fund, we have an excellent test case. Both the Mexico Fund and the iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW) have been in business since the 1990s, offering nearly 30 years of real-world results to compare. And, to the Mexico Fund's credit, it has slightly outperformed its benchmark ETF over that long span on a total return basis:

The Mexico Fund has a similar portfolio mix to the Mexico ETF. They currently share seven of the same top 10 holdings. Mexico Fund has 67% of its assets in its top 10 positions, compared to 61% for the iShares MSCI Mexico ETF, indicating slightly more portfolio concentration. Similarly, Mexico Fund has 32 total positions, compared to 48 for the iShares MSCI Mexico ETF. Mexico Fund also has an overweighted position in the consumer defensives sector, at 35% of the portfolio, which could help insulate in a global economic slowdown but lead to a touch of underperformance in a roaring Mexican bull market.

Regardless, these differences are all pretty small at the margins. And, as the long-term performance chart above shows, while there is enough difference from the index to produce a bit of alpha, the correlation between the Mexico Fund and Mexico ETFs should remain strong.

Mexico Fund's Discount To Assets

According to CEFConnect.com, the Mexico Fund was trading at a 20% discount to its net asset value ("NAV") as of April 16th, 2025. As such, purchasers of the Mexico Fund are buying a fine mix of Mexican assets at a substantial discount to their fair value, implying significant outperformance in the future.

The realization of this value may not play out any time soon, however.

I'd note that over the past year, Mexico Fund's discount to NAV has varied between 19% and 23%. That is a small range which implies that the market has consistently viewed Mexico Fund as deserving to trade at a roughly 20% discount to that NAV, and the discount level has been steady since 2023. If you go back to 2022, at that point MXF was only at a 14% discount to NAV. We could certainly see the NAV gap shrink by a few points going forward which would marginally boost MXF's returns compared to a Mexico ETF such as EWW.

However, you'd have to go all the way back to 2015 to find a time when MXF traded at no discount to its NAV. And since 2016, there has been an almost continuous double-digit gap between NAV and the Mexico Fund's market price. All this to say that while the current 20% discount is relatively large for a CEF, I am not particularly optimistic that this will turn into a profitable arbitrage opportunity anytime soon.

Mexico Fund Bottom Line

The Mexico Fund has outperformed the most popular ETF that gets exposure to the same asset class over a lengthy time period. That's a worthy achievement and makes Mexico Fund worthy of consideration in my view. The portfolio, while not dramatically different from the broad Mexico large-cap index, is slightly different enough that it could produce some additional alpha at the margins going forward.

There's also the possibility that the NAV discount will close in the future, which could help supercharge further returns. I wouldn't personally bet on the NAV gap shrinking meaningfully in the near term, though.

That said, I am bullish on Mexico, and this fund has proven its mettle over the long haul. For investors that prefer a CEF approach to this asset class, MXF is a fine pick.
